generated_note:
**Subjective**
Chief Complaint: Fatigue, low energy, headaches, elevated blood pressure, elevated A1C, declining kidney function, numbness and pins-and-needles in right toes, nocturia, exertional dyspnea, back pain

**History of Present Illness**
Patient presents with fatigue, low energy, and headaches for approximately four months. Reports headaches described as pressure sensation in the back of his head, three to four times per week. BP today was 158/94, which is above target. Reports checking at home a couple times a week and says it's been running in the 140s/85-90, sometimes higher. On amlodipine 10mg and losartan 100mg, both in the morning, and he's taking them every day without fail. Target for him given his diabetes and kidney disease is under 130/80, so we've got work to do. A1C came back at 8.1, up from 7.6 last time — so it's drifted in the wrong direction. On metformin 1000mg twice daily and Tresiba insulin 22 units at bedtime. He's checking fasting blood sugars at home and they're running 150-160, sometimes higher. He's not checking post-meal sugars. Admitted the holiday season was rough — diet fell off significantly, lots of sweets and parties — though he says he's been doing better the last couple of weeks. Kidney numbers: Creatinine is 1.8, up from 1.5 last visit. GFR is 42, down from 51 — so a meaningful drop over four months, faster than I'd like to see. That puts him in stage 3B CKD. Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio is 220, elevated. He had a recent visit with Dr. Khatri, his nephrologist, who was concerned about the protein and mentioned potentially starting an SGLT2 inhibitor — dapagliflozin — and wanted to coordinate with me first. I reviewed her note and I'm in agreement. Cholesterol panel: LDL is 98, which is okay but for his risk level I want it under 70. Triglycerides are 210, elevated, likely partly diet-related. On physical exam today: weight is 234 pounds, up 6 pounds from last visit. Height is five-ten, BMI approximately 33.5. Blood pressure as noted 158/94. Heart sounds regular, no murmurs. Lungs clear. No ankle edema — which is reassuring given the kidney function. Pedal pulses intact bilaterally. No open sores or calluses on the feet. However, he reports numbness and pins-and-needles sensation in the right toes for the past few months. On monofilament testing, sensation was diminished on the right foot and intact on the left — consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy. He also reports nocturia, up two to three times per night, no hematuria. He also mentioned mild exertional dyspnea with yard work, nothing severe. No chest pain.

**Objective**
**Physical Examination**
General: Weight 234 pounds, height 5'10", BMI approximately 33.5, BP 158/94, heart sounds regular, lungs clear, no ankle edema, pedal pulses intact bilaterally, no open sores or calluses on the feet, numbness and pins-and-needles sensation in the right toes for the past few months, diminished sensation on right foot on monofilament testing, nocturia, mild exertional dyspnea with yard work, no chest pain

**Assessment**
{{condition:type 2 diabetes}}, {{condition:hypertension}}, {{condition:chronic kidney disease}}, stage 3B CKD, diabetic peripheral neuropathy

**Plan**
1. Add hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily
2. Reduce metformin from 1000mg twice daily to 500mg twice daily
3. Increase Tresiba from 22 units at bedtime to 26 units at bedtime, titrate up by 2 units every three days until fasting sugars are under 130
4. Start dapagliflozin 10mg daily
5. Increase atorvastatin from 40mg to 80mg
6. Referred to podiatry for regular foot exams
7. Counseled on importance of foot care given neuropathy and diabetes
8. Referred to ophthalmology for annual diabetic retinopathy screening
9. Placed nutrition referral for refresher visit focused on diabetes, CKD, and hypertension
10. Counseled on low sodium diet under 2,000mg, moderate protein, and controlled carbohydrates
11. Stopped ibuprofen due to CKD
12. Switch to acetaminophen, up to 2,000mg per day
13. Educated on interconnected nature of conditions - elevated BP accelerating kidney decline, poor sugar control damaging kidneys further, and cycle to break
14. Encouraged use of wife's support with diet and foot checks
15. Discussed dialysis - not imminent at current GFR, but trend concerning, aggressive management can slow progression, many patients at his stage never reach dialysis
16. Labs ordered in four weeks: BMP, A1C, lipids, urine protein, and CBC
17. Follow-up appointment in six weeks
18. New prescriptions sent electronically to pharmacy
19. After-visit summary and updated medication list printed for him
